summary: This study identifies a novel ISG15 isoform (ncISG15) generated through alternative transcription that potently restricts influenza A virus infection by inhibiting polymerase activity. Unlike canonical ISG15, ncISG15 retains antiviral activity despite NS1-mediated counter-suppression, representing a naturally-occurring interferon-inducible restriction mechanism with ISGylation-independent antiviral mechanisms.

key_molecular_findings.mechanism: N-terminally truncated ISG15 isoform potently inhibits influenza A virus polymerase activity through ISGylation-independent mechanism
Canonical ISG15 isoform inhibits polymerase but can be reversed by NS1
ncISG15 remains less susceptible to NS1-mediated suppression due to stoichiometric differences and distinct ISGylation pattern

experimental_approach: Long-read RNA sequencing to identify novel ISG15 transcripts, molecular characterization of the ncISG15 isoform (5' UTR and N-terminal truncation), polymerase inhibition assays, ISGylation pattern analysis, overexpression and knockdown studies in infected cells, and NS1 counter-suppression assays.
evidence_quality: strong — Novel transcript discovery via unbiased long-read sequencing, functional validation through overexpression/knockdown studies showing restriction only with ncISG15, biochemical demonstration of polymerase inhibition, and mechanistic characterization of differential NS1 susceptibility
hpmi_relevance: Exemplifies host antiviral factor discovery through transcriptomic approaches, revealing how hosts generate naturally-occurring viral restriction factors. Demonstrates alternative isoform switching as an antiviral strategy that partially escapes viral immune evasion mechanisms (NS1). Highlights the complexity of ISG15-mediated antiviral immunity and structural variants that enhance restriction.
recommendation_reason: Represents cutting-edge discovery of a naturally-occurring host restriction mechanism with direct therapeutic implications. The identification that ncISG15 escapes NS1-mediated antagonism while canonical ISG15 does not provides novel insights into IAV-host evolutionary dynamics and identifies a potent antiviral target resistant to known viral counter-measures.
